papers.title: Research on Structural Variation Detection Methods of Wheat Genome Based on Deep Learning
papers.abstract: Due to the complexity of genome structure and technical conditions, wheat genome structure variation has not yet been comprehensively and accurately detected and evaluated for genetic effects. The aim of this study is to construct a method based on deep learning algorithm to accurately detect genomic structure variation in wheat. The method converts genomic data into image form by genomic structure variation image generation algorithm. A gene structure variation prediction model is constructed based on deep learning, and efficient and accurate structure variation prediction is realized by auto
papers.journal: Journal of Combinatorial Mathematics and Combinatorial Computing
papers.summary: This study develops a deep learning method to detect structural variations in the wheat genome by converting genomic data into images and using a prediction model. The approach aims to improve accuracy and comprehensiveness in identifying structural variants, which have been challenging to detect due to genome complexity.

papers.title: Tradeoffs in alignment and assembly-based methods for structural variant detection with long-read sequencing data
papers.abstract: Long-read sequencing offers long contiguous DNA fragments, facilitating diploid genome assembly and structural variant (SV) detection. Efficient and robust algorithms for SV identification are crucial with increasing data availability. Alignment-based methods, favored for their computational efficiency and lower coverage requirements, are prominent. Alternative approaches, relying solely on available reads for de novo genome assembly and employing assembly-based tools for SV detection via comparison to a reference genome, demand significantly more computational resources. However, the lack of
papers.summary: This study compares alignment-based and assembly-based methods for detecting structural variants (SVs) from long-read sequencing data, finding that while alignment-based methods are computationally efficient and require less coverage, assembly-based methods may offer advantages in certain contexts. The authors highlight tradeoffs between accuracy, computational cost, and coverage requirements, providing guidance for researchers choosing SV detection strategies.

papers.title: Haplotype-resolved assembly of auto-polyploid genomes via combining Hi-C and gametic data
papers.abstract: Haplotype-resolved genome assembly plays a crucial role in understanding allele-specific functions. However, obtaining haplotype-resolved assembly for auto-polyploid genomes remains challenging. Existing methods can be classified into reference-based phasing, assembly-based phasing, and gamete binning. Nevertheless, there is a lack of cost-effective and efficient methods for haplotyping auto-polyploid genomes. In this study, we propose a novel phasing algorithm called PolyGH, which combines Hi-C and gametic data. We conducted experiments on tetraploid potato cultivars and divided the method in
papers.summary: This paper introduces PolyGH, a new algorithm that combines Hi-C and gametic data to achieve cost-effective, haplotype-resolved genome assembly for auto-polyploid organisms. Tested on tetraploid potato cultivars, it addresses a key gap in existing methods for phasing complex polyploid genomes.

papers.title: Pasa: leveraging population pangenome graph to scaffold prokaryote genome assemblies
papers.abstract: Abstract Whole genome sequencing has increasingly become the essential method for studying the genetic mechanisms of antimicrobial resistance and for surveillance of drug-resistant bacterial pathogens. The majority of bacterial genomes sequenced to date have been sequenced with Illumina sequencing technology, owing to its high-throughput, excellent sequence accuracy, and low cost. However, because of the short-read nature of the technology, these assemblies are fragmented into large numbers of contigs, hindering the obtaining of full information of the genome. We develop Pasa, a graph-based al
papers.summary: Pasa is a new tool that uses population pangenome graphs to scaffold fragmented prokaryote genome assemblies, improving contiguity by leveraging structural variation across related strains. This addresses a key limitation of short-read sequencing, enabling more complete bacterial genomes for applications like antimicrobial resistance surveillance.

papers.title: Pasa: Leverage population pangenome graph to scaffold prokaryote genome assemblies
papers.abstract: Whole genome sequencing has increasingly become the essential method for studying the genetic mechanisms of antimicrobial resistance and for surveillance of drug-resistant bacterial pathogens. The majority of bacterial genomes sequenced to date have been sequenced with Illumina sequencing technology, owing to its high-throughput, excellent sequence accuracy, and low cost. However, because of the short-read nature of the technology, these assemblies are fragmented into large numbers of contigs, hindering the obtaining of full information of the genome. We develop Pasa, a graph-based algorithm t
papers.summary: Pasa is a new graph-based algorithm that uses population pangenome graphs to scaffold fragmented prokaryote genome assemblies, particularly those from short-read sequencing like Illumina. This approach helps overcome the limitation of fragmented assemblies by leveraging shared genomic information across related strains, enabling more complete genome reconstruction for antimicrobial resistance studies.

papers.title: Localized assembly for long reads enables genome-wide analysis of repetitive regions at single-base resolution in human genomes
papers.abstract: Background Long-read sequencing technologies have the potential to overcome the limitations of short reads and provide a comprehensive picture of the human genome. However, the characterization of repetitive sequences by reconstructing genomic structures at high resolution solely from long reads remains difficult. Here, we developed a localized assembly method (LoMA) that constructs highly accurate consensus sequences (CSs) from long reads. Methods We developed LoMA by combining minimap2, MAFFT, and our algorithm, which classifies diploid haplotypes based on structural variants and CSs. Using
papers.summary: Researchers developed LoMA, a localized assembly method that constructs highly accurate consensus sequences from long reads, enabling genome-wide analysis of repetitive regions at single-base resolution in human genomes. This approach overcomes limitations of short reads and improves characterization of repetitive sequences by combining existing tools with a new algorithm for classifying diploid haplotypes.

papers.title: Algorithm for DNA sequence assembly by quantum annealing
papers.abstract: The assembly task is an indispensable step in sequencing genomes of new organisms and studying structural genomic changes. In recent years, the dynamic development of next-generation sequencing (NGS) methods raises hopes for making whole-genome sequencing a fast and reliable tool used, for example, in medical diagnostics. However, this is hampered by the slowness and computational requirements of the current processing algorithms, which raises the need to develop more efficient algorithms. One possible approach, still little explored, is the use of quantum computing. We present a proof of conc
papers.summary: This paper explores the use of quantum annealing, a quantum computing approach, to solve the DNA sequence assembly problem more efficiently than classical algorithms. The authors present a proof-of-concept algorithm that maps the assembly task onto a quantum annealing framework, aiming to address the computational bottlenecks of current next-generation sequencing processing methods. This work highlights a novel but still early-stage application of quantum computing to a fundamental bioinformatics challenge.

papers.title: GAMBIT (Genomic Approximation Method for Bacterial Identification and Tracking): A methodology to rapidly leverage whole genome sequencing of bacterial isolates for clinical identification
papers.abstract: Whole genome sequencing of clinical bacterial isolates has the potential to transform the fields of medicine and public health, with particular impact on molecular epidemiology, infection control and assessing the spread of antibiotic resistance. To realize this potential, bioinformatic software needs to be developed that meets the quality standards of a diagnostic test to allow the reporting of identification results. Our research group has developed a methodology (GAMBIT: Genomic Approximation Method for Bacterial Identification and Tracking) using k-mer based strategies for identification o
papers.summary: GAMBIT is a new k-mer based bioinformatics method that uses whole genome sequencing data to rapidly and accurately identify clinical bacterial isolates, meeting diagnostic quality standards. This approach could improve molecular epidemiology, infection control, and antibiotic resistance tracking in clinical settings.

papers.title: A novel high-accuracy genome assembly method utilizing a high-throughput workflow
papers.abstract: Across domains of biological research using genome sequence data, high-quality reference genome sequences are essential for characterizing genetic variation and understanding the genetic basis of phenotypes. However, the construction of genome assemblies for various species is often hampered by complexities of genome organization, especially repetitive and complex sequences, leading to mis-assembly and missing regions. Here, we describe a high-throughput gold standard genome assembly workflow using a large-scale bacterial artificial chromosome (BAC) library with a refined two-step pooling stra
papers.summary: This paper presents a new genome assembly method that uses a high-throughput BAC library with a refined two-step pooling strategy to overcome challenges from repetitive and complex sequences, producing high-accuracy reference genomes. The workflow addresses common issues like mis-assembly and missing regions, offering a gold standard approach for diverse species.

papers.title: A Near Chromosome Assembly of the Dromedary Camel Genome
papers.abstract: The dromedary camel is an economically and socially important species of livestock in many parts of the world, being used for transport and the production of milk and meat. Much like cattle and horses, the camel may be found in industrial farming conditions as well as used in sporting. Camel racing is a multi-million dollar industry, with some specimens being valued at upward of 9.5 million USD. Despite its apparent value to humans, the dromedary camel is a neglected species in genomics. While cattle and other domesticated species have had much attention in terms of genome assembly, the camel
papers.summary: This study presents a near chromosome-level genome assembly for the dromedary camel, a species that has been relatively neglected in genomics despite its economic and cultural importance. The improved assembly provides a valuable genomic resource for future research on camel biology, breeding, and adaptation.

papers.title: A stochastic de novo assembly algorithm for viral-sized genomes obtains correct genomes and builds consensus
papers.summary: This paper presents a new stochastic algorithm for de novo assembly of viral-sized genomes that can accurately reconstruct complete genomes and build consensus sequences. The method addresses challenges in viral genome assembly by using a probabilistic approach to handle sequencing errors and repetitive regions.

papers.title: ChopStitch: exon annotation and splice graph construction using transcriptome assembly and whole genome sequencing data
papers.abstract: Motivation: Sequencing studies on non‐model organisms often interrogate both genomes and transcriptomes with massive amounts of short sequences. Such studies require de novo analysis tools and techniques, when the species and closely related species lack high quality reference resources. For certain applications such as de novo annotation, information on putative exons and alternative splicing may be desirable. Results: Here we present ChopStitch, a new method for finding putative exons de novo and constructing splice graphs using an assembled transcriptome and whole genome shotgun sequencing
papers.summary: ChopStitch is a new computational method that identifies putative exons and constructs splice graphs de novo using assembled transcriptome and whole genome sequencing data, without requiring a high-quality reference genome. This tool is particularly useful for non-model organisms where reference resources are lacking, enabling better annotation of exon structures and alternative splicing events.

papers.title: HapCUT2: robust and accurate haplotype assembly for diverse sequencing technologies
papers.abstract: Many tools have been developed for haplotype assembly—the reconstruction of individual haplotypes using reads mapped to a reference genome sequence. Due to increasing interest in obtaining haplotype-resolved human genomes, a range of new sequencing protocols and technologies have been developed to enable the reconstruction of whole-genome haplotypes. However, existing computational methods designed to handle specific technologies do not scale well on data from different protocols. We describe a new algorithm, HapCUT2, that extends our previous method (HapCUT) to handle multiple sequencing tech
papers.summary: HapCUT2 is a new algorithm for haplotype assembly that works robustly across diverse sequencing technologies, including Illumina, PacBio, and 10X Genomics linked reads. It extends the previous HapCUT method to handle different data types without sacrificing accuracy or scalability, enabling whole-genome haplotype reconstruction from various protocols.

papers.title: Fast-SG: an alignment-free algorithm for hybrid assembly
papers.abstract: Long read sequencing technologies are the ultimate solution for genome repeats, allowing near reference level reconstructions of large genomes. However, long read de novo assembly pipelines are computationally intense and require a considerable amount of coverage, thereby hindering their broad application to the assembly of large genomes. Alternatively, hybrid assembly methods which combine short and long read sequencing technologies can reduce the time and cost required to produce de novo assemblies of large genomes. In this paper, we propose a new method, called FAST-SG, which uses a new ult
papers.summary: FAST-SG is a new alignment-free algorithm for hybrid genome assembly that combines short and long sequencing reads more efficiently than existing methods. By avoiding computationally expensive alignment steps, it reduces the time and cost required to assemble large genomes, making hybrid assembly more accessible.

papers.title: Recycler: an algorithm for detecting plasmids from de novo assembly graphs
papers.abstract: Plasmids are central contributors to microbial evolution and genome innovation. Recently, they have been found to have important roles in antibiotic resistance and in affecting production of metabolites used in industrial and agricultural applications. However, their characterization through deep sequencing remains challenging, in spite of rapid drops in cost and throughput increases for sequencing. Here, we attempt to ameliorate this situation by introducing a new plasmid-specific assembly algorithm, leveraging assembly graphs provided by a conventional de novo assembler and alignments of pai
papers.summary: Recycler is a new algorithm that identifies plasmid sequences from microbial DNA sequencing data by analyzing assembly graphs produced by standard de novo assemblers, using paired-end read alignments to distinguish plasmids from chromosomal DNA. This tool addresses the challenge of characterizing plasmids, which are important for antibiotic resistance and industrial applications, by improving detection from deep sequencing data.

papers.title: A Short Sequence Splicing Method for Genome Assembly Using a Three-Dimensional Mixing-Pool of BAC Clones and High-throughput Technology
papers.abstract: Current genome sequencing techniques are expensive, and it is still a major challenge to obtain an individual whole-genome sequence. To reduce the cost of sequencing, this paper presents a high-throughput sequencing strategy using a three-dimensional mixing-pool, which is based on the cube. BAC clones are injected into each vertex of the cube, and sequencing of each plane provides information for multiple clones, thereby significantly reducing the cost of sequencing. In addition, Velvet was used to assemble sequencing data. The scaffold generated from Velvet contain a number of contigs, but th
papers.summary: This paper presents a cost-saving genome sequencing strategy using a three-dimensional mixing-pool of BAC clones arranged in a cube, where sequencing each plane provides information for multiple clones simultaneously. The approach uses Velvet for assembly, but the resulting scaffolds still contain many contigs, indicating limitations in the assembly quality.

papers.title: A novel method of consensus pan-chromosome assembly and large-scale comparative analysis reveal the highly flexible pan-genome of Acinetobacter baumannii
papers.abstract: Infections by pan-drug resistant Acinetobacter baumannii plague military and civilian healthcare systems. Previous A. baumannii pan-genomic studies used modest sample sizes of low diversity and comparisons to a single reference genome, limiting our understanding of gene order and content. A consensus representation of multiple genomes will provide a better framework for comparison. A large-scale comparative study will identify genomic determinants associated with their diversity and adaptation as a successful pathogen. We determine draft-level genomic sequence of 50 diverse military isolates a
papers.summary: This study presents a new method for constructing a consensus pan-chromosome assembly and applies it to 50 diverse military isolates of Acinetobacter baumannii, revealing a highly flexible pan-genome. The large-scale comparative analysis identifies genomic determinants associated with the pathogen's diversity and adaptation, overcoming limitations of previous studies that relied on single reference genomes and low-diversity samples.

papers.title: A Memory Efficient Short Read De Novo Assembly Algorithm
papers.abstract: : Sequencing the whole genome of various species has many applications, not only in understanding biological systems, but also in medicine, pharmacy, and agriculture. In recent years, the emergence of high-throughput next generation sequencing technologies has dramatically reduced the time and costs for whole genome sequencing. These new technologies provide ultrahigh throughput with a lower per-unit data cost. However, the data are generated from very short fragments of DNA. Thus, it is very important to develop algorithms for merging these fragments. One method of merging these fragments wit
papers.summary: This paper presents a memory-efficient algorithm for assembling short DNA sequence reads into longer contiguous sequences, addressing a key computational challenge posed by high-throughput sequencing technologies. The method reduces memory usage while maintaining assembly accuracy, enabling whole genome assembly on standard computing hardware. This work is important for making genome assembly more accessible and scalable as sequencing data volumes continue to grow.

papers.title: Oxford Nanopore sequencing, hybrid error correction, and de novo assembly of a eukaryotic genome
papers.abstract: Monitoring the progress of DNA molecules through a membrane pore has been postulated as a method for sequencing DNA for several decades. Recently, a nanopore-based sequencing instrument, the Oxford Nanopore MinION, has become available that we used for sequencing the S. cerevisiae genome. To make use of these data, we developed a novel open-source hybrid error correction algorithm Nanocorr (https://github.com/jgurtowski/nanocorr) specifically for Oxford Nanopore reads, as existing packages were incapable of assembling the long read lengths (5-50kbp) at such high error rate (between ∼5 and 40%
papers.summary: This paper presents the first sequencing of a eukaryotic genome (S. cerevisiae) using the Oxford Nanopore MinION, demonstrating its ability to generate very long reads (5-50 kbp) despite high error rates (5-40%). The authors developed a new open-source hybrid error correction algorithm, Nanocorr, to correct these error-prone reads using high-accuracy short reads, enabling successful de novo genome assembly. This work established a foundational approach for using long-read nanopore sequencing in genomics.

papers.title: De novo finished 2.8 Mbp Staphylococcus aureus genome assembly from 100 bp short and long range paired-end reads
papers.summary: This paper presents a method to assemble a complete 2.8 Mbp Staphylococcus aureus genome using only 100 bp short reads and long-range paired-end data, achieving a finished assembly without long-read sequencing. The approach demonstrates that combining short reads with long-range information can produce high-quality genomes, reducing the need for more expensive sequencing technologies.

papers.title: Efficient Synergistic Single-Cell Genome Assembly
papers.abstract: As the vast majority of all microbes are unculturable, single-cell sequencing has become a significant method to gain insight into microbial physiology. Single-cell sequencing methods, currently powered by multiple displacement genome amplification (MDA), have passed important milestones such as finishing and closing the genome of a prokaryote. However, the quality and reliability of genome assemblies from single cells are still unsatisfactory due to uneven coverage depth and the absence of scattered chunks of the genome in the final collection of reads caused by MDA bias. In this work, our ne
papers.summary: This paper presents a new computational method to improve genome assembly from single microbial cells by addressing biases caused by multiple displacement amplification (MDA). The approach synergistically combines data to reduce uneven coverage and fill in missing genome regions, leading to more complete and reliable assemblies for unculturable microbes.

papers.title: Comparative study of de novo assembly and genome-guided assembly strategies for transcriptome reconstruction based on RNA-Seq
papers.abstract: Transcriptome reconstruction is an important application of RNA-Seq, providing critical information for further analysis of transcriptome. Although RNA-Seq offers the potential to identify the whole picture of transcriptome, it still presents special challenges. To handle these difficulties and reconstruct transcriptome as completely as possible, current computational approaches mainly employ two strategies: de novo assembly and genome-guided assembly. In order to find the similarities and differences between them, we firstly chose five representative assemblers belonging to the two classes re
papers.summary: This study compares de novo and genome-guided assembly strategies for transcriptome reconstruction from RNA-Seq data, evaluating five representative assemblers. The findings highlight the strengths and limitations of each approach, providing guidance for selecting appropriate methods based on data quality and research goals.

papers.title: Arapan-S: a fast and highly accurate whole-genome assembly software for viruses and small genomes
papers.abstract: Genome assembly is considered to be a challenging problem in computational biology, and has been studied extensively by many researchers. It is extremely difficult to build a general assembler that is able to reconstruct the original sequence instead of many contigs. However, we believe that creating specific assemblers, for solving specific cases, will be much more fruitful than creating general assemblers. In this paper, we present Arapan-S, a whole-genome assembly program dedicated to handling small genomes. It provides only one contig (along with the reverse complement of this contig) in m
papers.summary: Arapan-S is a new genome assembly software designed specifically for small genomes and viruses, aiming to reconstruct a single complete contig rather than multiple fragments. The authors argue that specialized assemblers for specific cases are more effective than general-purpose assemblers.

papers.title: An MCMC algorithm for haplotype assembly from whole-genome sequence data.
papers.abstract: In comparison to genotypes, knowledge about haplotypes (the combination of alleles present on a single chromosome) is much more useful for whole-genome association studies and for making inferences about human evolutionary history. Haplotypes are typically inferred from population genotype data using computational methods. Whole-genome sequence data represent a promising resource for constructing haplotypes spanning hundreds of kilobases for an individual. In this article, we propose a Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) algorithm, HASH (haplotype assembly for single human), for assembling haploty
papers.summary: This paper presents a new Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) algorithm called HASH that assembles haplotypes from whole-genome sequence data for a single individual. Haplotypes are more informative than genotypes for genetic studies, and this method improves the ability to construct long-range haplotypes from sequencing reads.

papers.title: Barnacle: An Assembly Algorithm for Clone-based Sequences of Whole Genomes
papers.abstract: We propose an assembly algorithm Barnacle for sequences generated by the clone-based approach. We illustrate our approach by assembling the human genome. Our novel method abandons the original physical-mapping-first framework. As we show, Barnacle more effectively resolves conflicts due to repeated sequences which is the main difficulty of the sequence assembly problem. In addition, we are able to detect inconsistencies in the underlying data. We present and compare our results on the December 2001 freeze of the public working draft of the human genome with NCBI's assembly (Build 28). The asse
papers.summary: This paper presents Barnacle, a new assembly algorithm designed for clone-based whole genome sequences, which abandons the traditional physical-mapping-first approach. The algorithm more effectively resolves conflicts caused by repeated sequences and can detect data inconsistencies, as demonstrated on the human genome. It offers an improved method for assembling complex genomes compared to existing approaches like NCBI's Build 28.

papers.title: A deep-learning-informed prior and Bayesian model for differential AP-MS interactome analysis
papers.abstract: Affinity-purification mass spectrometry (AP-MS) maps a bait proteins partners, but every purification also captures abundant non-specific background that masks genuine interactors. Established tools such as SAINTexpress and CompPASS score one evidence type, treat correlated signals as independent, and ignore prior knowledge of likely interactions. BayesInteractomics, an open-source Julia framework, addresses both limitations by combining machine learning with Bayesian statistics. A neural network trained on protein structures predicts direct binding. A calibrated meta-learner turns this into a
papers.summary: This paper introduces BayesInteractomics, a new computational framework that combines deep learning with Bayesian statistics to improve the analysis of affinity-purification mass spectrometry (AP-MS) data. By using a neural network trained on protein structures to predict direct binding and incorporating this as prior knowledge, the method outperforms existing tools in identifying genuine protein-protein interactions while reducing false positives from non-specific background.

papers.title: scJET: Full-gene Space Single-cell Expression Generation with Patch-based Transformer Modeling
papers.abstract: Most single-cell generative models rely on highly variable genes (HVGs) or low-dimensional latent representations, limiting their capacity to capture the complexity of full-gene features. We present scJET, a patch-based Transformer denoising framework that operates in full-gene space. scJET preserves global manifold structure, local neighborhood statistics, and gene-level expression programs. By combining scalable patch tokenization with full-gene denoising, scJET provides an efficient framework for transcriptome-wide single-cell matrix generation.
papers.summary: scJET is a new deep learning method that uses a patch-based Transformer to generate and denoise single-cell gene expression data across the entire transcriptome, not just a subset of highly variable genes. This approach better preserves the complex structure of gene expression programs and relationships between cells, offering a more comprehensive framework for single-cell data analysis.

papers.title: SPECTER-Based Semantic Triage of Biomedical Literature for Systematic Reviews in Mutational Signature Analysis
papers.abstract: Systematic reviews in computational biology require screening large heterogeneous bibliographic sets, especially when topics span computational methods, cancer genomics and statistical modelling. This paper presents a reproducible semantic triage pipeline that combines SPECTER scientific-document embeddings, research-question similarity, proposal-summary similarity and domain keyword coverage to rank candidate studies for systematic review screening. The pipeline was evaluated on 2,231 Covidence records, including 120 final included studies (prevalence = 5.38%), against keyword-only, TF-IDF, B
papers.summary: This paper introduces a reproducible pipeline that uses SPECTER embeddings and similarity metrics to automatically rank biomedical literature for systematic reviews, specifically for mutational signature analysis. Tested on over 2,200 records, the method improves screening efficiency by prioritizing relevant studies, reducing manual effort in systematic reviews.

papers.title: BBBP_Atlas: Unified Interpretable Modeling of Blood Brain Barrier Permeability across Small Molecules and Peptides
papers.abstract: Accurate prediction of blood-brain barrier permeability (BBBP) is essential for central nervous system drug discovery, yet existing models are often limited by their reliance on predefined physicochemical descriptors, small-molecule-centered training sets, or conformation-dependent representations, which restricts their transferability across chemically diverse modalities especially peptides. In addition, publicly available BBBP datasets remain fragmented, inconsistently standardized, and weakly controlled for molecular redundancy, increasing the risk of data leakage and overestimated model pe
papers.summary: The authors introduce BBBP_Atlas, a unified and interpretable deep learning model that predicts blood-brain barrier permeability for both small molecules and peptides, overcoming limitations of prior models that were restricted to small molecules or required predefined descriptors. They also compile and standardize fragmented public BBBP datasets with improved redundancy control to reduce data leakage and overestimation of model performance.

papers.title: Mutational bias shapes protein evolution across RNA viruses
papers.abstract: Mutational biases can influence genome composition, but their contribution to protein evolution remains difficult to quantify. Here we utilize a nearly neutral framework that translates nucleotide mutational spectra into expected amino acid substitution patterns and equilibrium amino acid compositions. Using SARS-CoV-2 as a model system, we show that the viral mutational spectrum explains more than 50% of the variation in observed single-nucleotide amino acid substitutions and predicts the overall direction of proteome-wide amino acid composition change during the COVID-19 pandemic. The predic
papers.summary: This study shows that mutational biases, not just natural selection, significantly shape protein evolution in RNA viruses. Using SARS-CoV-2, the authors demonstrate that the virus's mutation spectrum explains over half of the observed amino acid changes and predicts shifts in protein composition during the pandemic.
